data.title: Wall Street Brunch: Tesla Earnings Steal The Spotlight
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify.

Will the numbers improve sentiment after disappointing robotaxi event? (0:17) Durable goods orders expected to drop. (2:16) Boeing has a tentative agreement with union to end strike. (2:56)

The following is an abridged transcript:

Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) earnings highlight this week as reporting season gains steam.

Analysts expect the EV maker to post revenue of $29.73 billion and EPS of $0.86 when it issues results on Wednesday. Gross margin is expected to come in at 18.77%.

For the conference call, a point of major emphasis will be Elon Musk’s commentary on the timing of a new mass-market cheaper electric vehicle, as well as the latest updates on FSD, energy storage business and robotaxi developments.

Seeking Alpha analyst Richard Durant, who has a Sell rating on the stock, says the business “faces a critical 12-18 month period, as its investments in self-driving technology are reaching a point where significant progress will be needed for the company to maintain its valuation premium.”

“This is a risky setup, as it is largely unknown, how self-driving performance will improve with access to more data, but the results aren't particularly encouraging so far.”

But in the bullish camp, Oliver Rodzianko says the recent “We, Robot” event was a success, despite the precipitant stock decline.

“Not only does it show Tesla investors and customers that the company's plans have a substantial grounding, but the company should be commended for how quickly it embraced a full pivot to autonomous taxis,” he said. “Although Musk previously announced that Tesla's robotaxis would be on the road by 2020, this doesn't diminish the groundbreaking achievement of being able to showcase a fully autonomous driving experience in 2024 in a state-of-the-art vehicle design.”

Also on the earnings calendar, on Monday, Nucor (NUE) and Logitech (LOGI) report.

General Electric (GE), Philip Morris (PM), Verizon (VZ), Texas Instruments (TXN), Lockheed Martin (LMT), General Motors (GM) and 3M (MMM) weigh in on Tuesday.

Joining Tesla on Wednesday are Coca-Cola (KO), T-Mobile (TMUS), IBM (IBM), AT&T (T) and Boeing (BA).

Valero (VLO), UPS (UPS), Honeywell (HON), American Airlines (AAL), Northrop Grumman (NOC), Southwest Airlines (LUV), and Dow (DOW) issue results on Thursday.

On Friday, Centene (CNC), HCA (HCA) and Colgate-Palmolive (CL) are up.

On the economic front, September durable goods orders will garner the most attention. The report hits Friday, with economists expecting a -0.9% decline in the headline number and a -0.1% drop in core orders, ex transportation.

Wells Fargo economists say: “Durable goods orders have been volatile in recent months primarily due to swings in aircraft orders.”

“The strong start to the Fed's easing cycle provides scope for a rebound in equipment spending in the year ahead,” they add. “That said, it will likely take some time for lower rates to filter through the economy and revitalize business demand for durable goods. Looking to September, a decline in industrial production foretells an underwhelming durable goods print.”

In the news this weekend, a union representing around 30,000 Boeing (BA) employees announced a tentative agreement that, if finalized, could end a more-than-month-long strike that has disrupted production.

As part of the deal, Boeing is set to offer its workers a 35% pay hike over four years, sharply higher than its initial offer of 25%, the International Association of Machinists said. Other terms of the deal include a reinstated incentive plan and improved contributions to employees' 401(k) plans.

“We look forward to our employees voting on the negotiated proposal,” Boeing said.

In addition, Boeing is deciding whether it will sell assets such as underperforming business units to raise cash as it seeks to overcome its recent crises. That’s according to The Wall Street Journal.

The company’s board met last week at the company’s headquarters in Arlington, Va., where they questioned division heads and combed through reports on the condition of each unit.

CEO Kelly Ortberg, who was brought in to fix the company’s operations in early August, wasn’t there for the discussions as he was in strike negotiations.

And happy hour is over.

TGI Friday’s, the bar/restaurant that is given credit for popularizing that term for discount drinks, is looking for financing as it prepares to file for bankruptcy in the coming weeks.

Bloomberg says the company is in talks with lenders to search for a loan that would let it continue running its restaurants through the Chapter 11 process and emerge as an operating company. TGI Friday’s joins the long list of casual dining chains struggling with consumers' shifting preference for fast-casual eateries like Chipotle (CMG).

The TGI Friday’s on Manhattan’s Upper East Side was one of the world’s first singles bar and it was also where Brian Flanagan (played by Tom Cruise) learned to bartend in “Cocktail.”

For income investors, companies that have an ex-dividend date coming next week include Caterpillar (CAT), CVS Health (CVS), Lowe’s (LOW), Clorox (CLX), and Fastenal (FAST).

Companies forecast to increase their quarterly dividend payouts include Smurfit Westrock (SW) to $0.33 from $0.3025, Hartford Financial Services Group (HIG) to $0.50 from $0.47, Entergy (ETR) to $1.20 from $1.13 and American Electric Power (AEP) to $0.92 from $0.88.

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, UBS Global Wealth Management said it now has an attractive view on U.S. equities, supported in part by growing adoption of artificial intelligence technology.

David Lefkowitz, head of equities Americas, says: “From a macro perspective, the combination of slowing but durable economic growth, healthy earnings growth, and continued Fed rate cuts are all supportive.”

While economic growth is slowing, the country’s labor market is “healthy,” with real wages rising, he said.

He lists his top picks, but it’s good to point out that UBS has had a neutral view on U.S. equities while the S&P 500 has risen more than 20% year to date.

Among the names are Meta (META), Home Depot (HD), Ralph Lauren (RL), Coca-Cola (KO), ExxonMobil (XOM), BofA (BAC), Merck (MRK), Microsoft (MSFT), Freeport-McMoRan (FCX), Equity Residential (EQR) and NextEra Energy (NEE).

data.title: A&W: Valuation Gets Ambitious As Risks Go Vertical
data.description:
Note: All values are in CAD unless noted otherwise. All stock prices refer to the TSX unless noted otherwise.

It is tough finding picks from a value investor's standpoint in this market. You have some quality stocks trading at some of the highest nosebleed levels. You also have lower quality stocks that appear cheap and then get slammed as management repeatedly fails to deliver. So patience is key in the hunt. After a lot of patience, we finally pivoted to buy ratings on A&W (TSX:AW:CA) and bought some shares.

The rationale was that the sound valuation and a complete insulation from key risks facing burger chains, made this a tasty snack. The stock delivered as management completed a rather unusual transaction, converting the royalty company into a quick-service-restaurant model.

Where Things Stand

One of the key things to understand here is that we felt A&W was attractive in its royalty format, because of two things. The first being that it sported a near 6.8% yield when we hit the buy ratings. Considering that A&W distributed all its earnings, we were looking at a 15X earnings multiple. That multiple was attractive as A&W had no labor costs risks and no risks of a margin compression. It also carried very little debt. Even the buyout paid some tribute to this thinking by assigning 41% of the new company ownership to the 3% A&W royalty rights.

Keeping things in perspective here, let's see what has happened. The stock has rocketed over $40.00 per share, even higher than the cash offer. So even on the old "lower-risk" model, we would get just a 4.8% yield versus the 6.8% where we gave this a green light. Considering the current economic conditions in Canada, we would give anything under a 6% yield as a "hold" and a 5% yield as a "sell". Again, all of that is on the old royalty model. But here we have pole vaulted into a bunch of new risks. Wage inflation is a clear and present danger, especially with the Government clamping down on some bad behavior by corporations to dodge wage hikes. Margin compression is almost certainly on its way in the next recession. The company has taken on a large load of debt at the same time.

▪ ~3.0x net debt (incl. net leases) / 2023 combined adjusted EBITDA(3), with up to $325 million revolving credit facility from CIBC and RBC ▪ Proceeds to be used to fund the cash consideration of the transaction and repay current indebtedness of the Fund and A&W Food Services.

Source: A&W

Q3-2024

The most recent results were the ones just prior to this transaction. They reflect the older low-risk model in its entirety. They were not pretty. Royalty income was up just 0.3% year over year in Q3-2024.

Royalty Pool Same Store Sales Growth is a function of changes in guest counts and check size, which are impacted by party size, menu prices and menu mix and changes in consumers discretionary spending. The Royalty Pool Same Store Sales Growth(i) of -1.0% for the third quarter and nil year to date primarily results from a decrease in guest counts partially offset by an increase in average check size due in part to industry-wide inflation on goods, services, and labour. The decrease in guest counts reflects A&W's proportionate share of an overall decline in traffic at burger quick service restaurants ("QSR") across Canada. Food Services believes the decline in traffic at A&W restaurants and burger QSR more generally is attributable to increased interest rates and inflation, along with market uncertainty, which have impacted consumer discretionary spending. In response to these economic conditions, Food Services continues to seek new and innovative ways to offer A&W's guests a delicious and affordable experience and in turn increase guest traffic.

Source: A&W

Some other key numbers from that press release are highlighted below.

To be fair, the above results do include $2.46 million of one time transaction costs related to this new setup. In the absence of that, the distribution would be covered. Nonetheless, the same store sales are pointing to some early signs of a pushback from the beaten consumer. We don't like using anecdotal evidence in general, but this is a place we dine at from time to time. The average price per person for a meal has doubled since 2020. Our experience is rather drastically different than what the management thinks about its current pricing.

Thomas Burke

Yes, hi, guys. Just -- well, a couple of things about the numbers. So are we seeing just obviously aside from the general market weakness, is there any idea about market share numbers? Is this really an industry thing? And how can we characterize this, because these numbers over the past few quarters are very uncharacteristic of the traditional A&W numbers since I've been following the company over 20 years?

Susan Senecal

Sure. I can comment on that. Thanks for the question. Yeah, obviously, I think the economic situation is part of the driver of this. We also noticed a really big desire for value and affordability and so on and so forth, which we need to address, it feels like, in a more deliberate way. So that's what we're focused on. And I think we're already starting to understand a little bit more about that need and how we might compete more effectively when it comes to giving even more value to our guests and making sure that for families and so on that we’re still very affordable. It's been a bit of a focus for the whole year, but we certainly are reemphasizing that in our -- in terms of our plans, especially going into this Q4.

Source: A&W Q3-2024 Conference Call Transcript, (emphasis ours)

We have certainly not seen this focus, and frankly we have seen far better value conscious pricing from Burger King (part of Restaurant Brands International Inc. (QSR)) and McDonald's Corporation (MCD), in Canada.

Verdict

We no longer own this.

Credit conditions are over easy today and debt is cheap. So it is possible the next 2-3 quarters don't exactly look bad under the new model. But unless there is some drastic price alignment, we think the core A&W customer is likely to run, or at least come in to dine less frequently. Optimistically, we would assign this a peak multiple of 16X potential sustainable earnings of about $2.00 per share. That gives us a fair value of $32.00 and even that assumes that they get some traction from the Pret Deal. An alternative valuation would be at 11X EV to EBITDA, using a base case of $90 million in EBITDA. These numbers give us a lower fair value of around $29.00 per share. We are starting the new shares at a Sell and will update our views once we see the next quarter's results.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult with a professional who knows their objectives and constraints.

data.title: DBP: Gold And Silver Exposure In One Wrapper
data.description:
Gold has been on absolute fire for the past year, unrelentingly making new highs as it continues to, I believe, send a warning about global debt and increasingly fragile monetary systems. More recently, poor man’s Gold, i.e. Silver, has also been suddenly running hot with some major technical resistance levels finally being broken. I remain bullish on both. And if you are, as well, then you may want to consider the Invesco DB Precious Metals Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:DBP). This is an ETF that aims to follow the DBIQ Optimum Yield Precious Metals Index Total Return™. This index tracks how futures contracts perform for Gold and Silver. The fund's main goal is to give investors a cheap and easy way to invest in the precious metals market.

What makes DBP stand out is how it uses the Optimum Yield™ method. This approach tries to lessen the negative effects of contango and boost the good effects of backwardation. In other words, it's meant to dynamically manage the roll aspect of the future contracts to enhance return depending upon the shape of the futures term structure, which can have long-term return implications most investors may not otherwise consider as either an opportunity or risk.

A Look At The Holdings

Roughly 80% of the fund is made up of Gold futures. The fund puts so much weight on gold because it matters a lot in the precious metals market and can help keep things steady when the economy gets rocky. The remaining futures allocation goes to Silver.

Keep in mind that these percentages show the fund's futures contracts. The fund puts its collateral into high-quality government securities. The largest collateral allocation goes to the Invesco Government & Agency Portfolio. This is a money market fund that puts money into U.S. government securities and repurchase agreements. Next comes US Treasury Bills, followed by a short-duration.

Peer Comparison

Some key competitors include the SPDR Gold Shares (GLD), iShares Silver Trust (SLV), and the abrdn Physical Precious Metals Basket Shares ETF (GLTR). What makes DBP stand out is how it deals with futures contracts instead of actual metals directly. To the extent that the fund’s approach can generate excess returns by managing the term structure of Gold and Silver, respectively, during periods of contango and backwardation, there is theoretically potential for these funds to do better than spot returns.

In practice? When we look at the performance of DBP to GLD, SLV, and GLTR, we find that DBP has outperformed GLTR, but not outright owning GLD or SLV, which invest directly in the metals as opposed to the futures side.

Pros and Cons

On the plus side, it’s convenient. In one ETF, you have Gold and Silver in one trade (through futures). Because the fund uses futures contracts, you don't have to stress about keeping, insuring, or moving actual metal, which can be a hassle when you own it yourself if you want to avoid funds like GLD or SLV. What's more, DBP's Optimum Yield™ method aims to boost returns by fine-tuning the rolling of futures contracts. This approach can be useful in markets experiencing contango or backwardation giving DBP an advantage over ETFs that just track spot prices or use simpler rolling strategies.

The collateral investments in safe, interest-bearing securities also provide an extra source of return, which can be valuable when yields are low. This setup allows investors to profit from both changes in commodity prices and interest income.

But we need to think about the downsides too. Using futures contracts makes things tricky and might cause tracking errors. The fund might not match gold and silver spot prices, which can puzzle investors used to ETFs that actually invest in Gold and Silver physically. Also, the futures approach can lead to different tax dynamics than ETFs with physical metals.

Conclusion

It’s an interesting fund and approach, but candidly I’d prefer just blending GLD and SLV directly. Get direct access to the metal as opposed to futures, and rebalance accordingly. Despite that being my preference, there’s nothing fundamentally wrong with the fund. I think if you want convenience, this is a good way to get easy exposure to Gold and Silver. Just know that futures will always be more complicated than outright owning the underlying assets.

data.title: Life360: I Love It As A User, But Has A Lot To Prove And Valuation Is Expensive
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Life360 (NASDAQ:LIF) is a recently IPO'd company that offers location services and is trying to develop a range of products that will keep users loyal to the brand. It has grown a lot and has some interesting ideas in development, but aren't yet fully proven and the valuation is high, so there's no margin for error and it would have to be executed perfectly to offer appealing returns.

Although I'm a regular user of the product, I understand its value proposition and where it wants to go, I still don't feel confident enough to assign a buy rating.

The Core of Life360

At the core of Life360 there's a platform for mobile phones and offers location services. The idea is that families and friends create groups and share their location in real time and, in my experience as a user of the app, I can tell you about some of the features of the app that I value the most:

  • Location alerts: You can select predetermined locations and name them as you like (work/home/etc) so that when one of the members of the group arrives at the place, Life360 will send a notification to the members of the group.
  • Driving reports: When a member is driving, the app alerts of this, shows the speed and an estimated time at which they will arrive at the predetermined destinations. It also shows the history of recent travels.
  • Assistance in case of emergencies: Luckily I've never needed this option, but if there's an emergency the application has an SOS button that alerts your contacts, as well as a vehicle accident detector.

This platform basically offers the peace of mind of knowing where your loved ones are in case they have a car accident or are in a risk situation. For example, when my sister goes out to a party, it's starting to get late and she doesn't answer messages, I can simply log into Life360 and find out if she's still at the party, if she's already on her way, or raise alarms if she's in some strange location.

The disadvantage is that this app only works with the internet, so if your cell phone runs out of battery you would no longer have access to the real-time location, although at least you could know its last location in case something unfortunate has happened. Simply put, it's easy for users to become regulars on a platform that offers this level of security for their loved ones.

Monetization

While I like this app and I was part of the "word of mouth" with my family and friends, at first look it wouldn't be clear how Life360 could monetize its users, since I don't think it's worth paying for just a location service (my opinion).

Given this, one of the strategies that the company has adopted has been to include hardware in its product catalog through acquisitions. The two acquisitions are the following:

  • Tile, a location device which was acquired in 2022. This can be used on cell phones, keys and other objects that you want to have located and works through Bluetooth.
  • Jiobit, acquired in 2022, uses secure cellular, GPS, Wi-Fi, and Bluetooth connections to pinpoint children, pets or the elderly. This is more secure since it can track anything with any available signal and doesn't depend only on Wi-Fi like Life360 or Bluetooth like Tile.

In 2023 the hardware represented 19% of total sales growing 21.5% compared to the previous year, but you still have to download the Tile app separately to use it, the ideal would be that in the near future both applications would be unified to have everything in one place and thus make it worth paying for a subscription. It's worth mentioning that in this space the company has a giant rival in Apple, which also has a tracker and a location app. Coming integrated with iPhone makes it much more convenient and reliable for users.

Regarding the platform, it currently has a freemium model in which you can access most of the functions but are somewhat limited compared to the Silver and Gold version. For example, with the free version you can see two days of location history, while with the silver version you have up to seven days and in the gold version up to a whole month.

I think that the company will have a hard job making this subscription as attractive as possible, since the functionalities to add to a platform are rather few with current capacity. However, I see it as very positive that the company will begin to place ads in the free version and you can avoid them by paying the subscription, which I do see as an interesting path because the platform is already made and the only thing they have to do is find a space to position ads, get advertisers and there they'll have revenue with wide margins.

As far as the future is concerned, I find very interesting what can be achieved in the "pets" segment and if the Tile functionalities are incorporated within the same application. As for what the company says, they see growth potential in auto insurance, family financial services and elder care which are usually recurring services and with high switching costs if the concept is proven correct.

As a fun fact, in the memberships Life360 is offering a Tile product as a gift. Also, I received this notification saying that if I pay for my gold membership they will give me six months of Headspace, a platform that provides guided meditation sessions. It's not of my special interest, but you can notice the efforts to make the membership attractive through functionality, hardware gifts and even partnerships.

High Growth, High Margin

Between 2018 and 2023, the company's revenue has grown at a very high 59% annually on average. This can be broken down into a growth in monthly active users of 28% annually and revenue per active user of 31%. For example, at the end of 2018 the company had 19M active users per month who spent an average of $1.69 dollars per year, while at the end of 2023 active users had grown to 62M and each one spent an average of $4.91 dollars.

The takeaway is that the company is growing in terms of user adoption and demonstrating that its platform is popular, but it's also getting users to spend more on memberships/hardware/services. Currently Snapchat has 800 million monthly users (but it's a social network so it's not entirely comparable) and it is estimated that Google Maps has 1,000 million monthly active users, showing that there's also potential to grow in this aspect considering the barely 62 million of Life360.

Life360's gross margins are quite high considering the software nature of the company, even considering hardware (which is typically low margin) the company has maintained an average gross margin of 75% and in the first two quarters of fiscal 2024 It has been 76%.

I don't think there's much room for expansion but I do think it can maintain this level for many years.

Another key note is that the company has already reached breakeven in EBITDA during Q2 2024, which sets the path for operating leverage due to the low costs of operating the business. The objective mentioned in the investor presentation is to reach margins of 25% EBITDA, which I consider really achievable.

Theoretically, the company is cash flow positive and in 2023 it generated $26 million of free cash flow, but this is because the stock-based compentsation was $39 million which is not a real cash flow, therefore I don't consider it has reached cash flow breakeven (although it's very close to achieving it).

Valuation

As we saw throughout the article, the company has opportunities to grow both in monthly active users and in revenue per user. For my estimate, users would increase about 20% annually until reaching 160 million and revenue per user would grow 15% annually until reaching almost $10 dollars per user. This coincides with the goal of reaching 150M active users and $1 billion in revenue.

This would result in sales growth of 35% annually, assuming the target EBITDA margins of 25% are reached (achievable considering the business is mostly software) and the company trades at a valuation multiple of 25 times EV/EBITDA, performance would be negative still. That is, it would be very overvalued currently from my point of view.

To obtain a return of 14% annually in the next five years, the company would have to grow revenue 50% annually with margins of 25% and a multiple of 30 times EV/EBITDA. It's not the most favorable situation to start since it would be priced to perfection. I'm not saying it's impossible, but it does seem like the safety margin is zero.

Risks

Beyond valuation, a big risk is that the company competes with many powerful brands. In localization there's Snapchat, which has the advantage of also being a social network, in devices there's Apple as I mentioned and of course there are many other apps to track family members because are very easy to develop.

Given this, the company must develop a powerful value proposition and brand power that makes users not want to change it, which remains to be proven and it's not guaranteed. If we add to this the demanding valuation, we have a formula for an IPO that may not necessarily be a failed one, but could be sideways for a while, as is the case of Air Bnb: A great company with growth but that went public at an expensive price and has been sideways since December 2020 (IPO date).

The Bottom Line

I love the company as a user, the freemium model allows you to develop affinity with the brand and a certain loyalty, but I'm still not entirely sure that the day will come when I decide to pay a subscription for Life360 when there are other ways to know the location of my loved ones, so the ad model is the income stream I'm most excited about going forward, along with elder/pet/child care which would have more value to users, but it's not yet fully developed.

Coupled with a rich valuation, I don't think now is the best time to invest in this company, so it would be best to give it a few years to mature and wait until it can be acquired at a reasonable valuation. As I mentioned, Air Bnb has shown me that an excellent company of which you are a regular user can remain flat for years. For this reason, my decision would be to hold or not buy.

data.title: Papa John's: Shift From Strategy To Value A Catalyst In 'Battle Of The Pizzas'
data.description:
The shift towards value means using higher caliber weapons in the 'Battle of the Pizzas'

If the 'Battle of the Steakhouses' seems to have a clear winner, in the 'Battle of the Pizzas' Domino's Pizza (DPZ) appears to be maintaining its lead. But Q3 is showing itself to be a quarter where guests are still cautious about the size of their checks and are trading down with the deepening of the value proposition of c-stores.

In this scenario, Papa John's (NASDAQ:PZZA) seemed to be the underdog. This was as long as it did not maintain a clear approach to its marketing strategy in relation to value. Since the appointment of Mark Shambura as CMO, Papa John's has maintained its focus on highlighting the brand's premium ingredients with the 'Better Ingredients, Better Pizza' campaign. It also developed a digital-first approach, deepening its own digital sales channels that are so important in this market segment.

Note that the changes in communication that previously tried to highlight fresh ingredients and more artisanal products ended up shifting their focus to entry price points, LTOs and value promotions. This was the case of Darden Brands' (DRI) Olive Garden, which after seeing its traffic drop, brought forward its 'Never Ending Pasta Bowl' value promotion and partnered with Uber Eats (UBER) for third-party deliveries on its own sales channels. All of this came together with a change in its communication, which now focuses more on the 'NEPB' entry prices and other promotions.

Those who can, keep their promotional mix lean while focusing on their premium ingredients. This was the case of fast-casual Shake Shack (SHAK) with its latest 'Worth It' campaign that aims to reach more occasions of middle/low-income guests, highlighting its value proposition through the difference in quality of its burgers compared to other QSRs. Now, unlike other restaurants, Shake Shack can hit that note again given its average demographic of $100,000 household income. The average check appears to be much more elastic than at other LSRs, keeping its comparable sales positive despite declining traffic. This seems to be the approach taken by other restaurants that didn't want to give in to the promotional environment to garner some incremental traffic like First Watch (FWRG).

Papa John’s is more similar to Olive Garden than Shake Shack. That is, while the CMO nailed the digital-first approach, it seems he waited too long to pivot his marketing toward value. The effect of this approach was that while premium guests continued to bring in a hefty check (especially with guests buying two or more pizzas), transactions for lower-ticket items dropped off precipitously, as did traffic overall. With Mr. Shamura leaving to take on the CMO role at Panera Bread, it was time to adjust the marketing mix to pivot toward value.

Making Papa John’s more value-conscious and guest-friendly seemed to be the no. 1 concern for new CEO Todd Penegor, the former CEO of Wendy’s (WEN) who stepped down from his previous role to allow Kirk Tanner to take over the fast-food giant. Mr. Penegor appears to be one of the few CEOs in the recent restaurant industry musical chairs who came from within the industry (only 30 percent of new CEOs have come from other restaurants). That’s an urgent need, given that comparable sales continue to decline, especially in the U.S. segment, and its main competitors have been ramping up their aggressive offerings, their marketing campaigns only widening the value-perception gap. Meanwhile, Papa John’s has continued to brag about the quality of its pizzas. Occasional guests knew it, so much so that they’ve been buying doubles of them. The fight has now moved on to the non-occasional guests.

Take Domino's as an example of success in the 'Battle of the Pizzas', with positive comparable sales throughout FY 2024 to date. The company is openly focusing its strategy on value promotions, seeking to target its promotional mix to guests with lower household incomes. This is the bulk of the guests that remain for QSRs and have not yet switched down (c-stores, bakeries and supermarkets). Examples of recent developments by Domino's include:

  • The recent 'MOREflation' campaign allowed guests to trade in a pizza that combined two or more two-topping pizzas for $6.99 for a free large pizza. The promotion ran until the end of September and made quite a splash;
  • The company revamped its Emergency Pizza promotion that debuted in late 2023 and quickly transitioned to a BOGO promotion. The company is currently offering customers a free medium pizza with two toppings for the future when they place an initial online order through January 19, 2025. The EP leveraged the chain’s revamped loyalty app, Domino’s Rewards, which recently turned one year old and is a key part of the company’s comparable sales growth strategy by supporting traffic resilience by leveraging the occasions when guests use Domino’s;
  • LTOs that drive traffic during a specific week like National Pizza Month saw sales skyrocket during the week of September 30 through October 6. This year, menu-priced pizzas were discounted by a whopping 50%;
  • In late 2023, the company also launched its 'Hungry For More' campaign, encouraging menu innovations that had been on the back burner after more intense promotions. Since then, Domino's has introduced macaroni and cheese bowls to the menu for the first time in two styles (Five-Cheese and Spicy Buffalo) as well as New York-style pizza, made with a thinner crust and a blend of provolone cheese.

Other pizza companies have also been deepening their value proposition with value promotions recently. Cici’s Pizza, for example, also launched an LTO from October 7 through Sunday through November 3 that served two one-topping pizzas for $6.99 each. Papa Murphy’s also recently launched a limited-time value platform (September 9 through November 12) that featured its three large pizzas for $7.99 each, as well as its recent Shredded Pepperoni, Crumbled Sausage, and Classic Cheese launches, and included a 30% discount for National Pizza Month in the same month as Domino’s.

At c-stores, price points are even lower, including some interesting menu innovations. Casey’s (CASY) followed Domino’s by announcing a 50% discount for a week during National Pizza Month and introduced the Jalapeño Popper Pizza. Casey’s is the fifth largest pizza chain in the United States. At Circle K, guests can get two personal-sized slices of pizza for $5. According to Technomic, the average price of a cheese pizza at a c-store is $6.63, while at an LSR pizza chain it’s around $13. That’s about a 50% difference!

Understanding that it really needs to play the price game to win back price-sensitive guests who have migrated to other chains in search of value promotions, Papa John's has relaunched its marketing proposition (now focused on value and at specific price points) with 'Better Get You Some'. In the meantime, developments have been seen, such as the deepening of its mix and match platform (Papa Pairings) from $6.99, the launch of the Cheesy Burger Pizza for $9.99 and the extra-large New York Style pizza for $10.99. At least during Q2 2024, we did not see a big difference in comparable sales, as Papa John's still lags behind when it comes to appeal. This is typical of companies that have had to redirect their promotional mix and end up spending incremental dollars on advertising to hit the target.

Observe the comparable sales situation for all segments in the following graph:

Persisting on this mistake has cost Papa John's dearly, especially in the United States. Note that in all periods analyzed, Papa John's ranks behind the LSR SSS Index, as does Pizza Hut, owned by Yum! Brands (YUM). In the period analyzed, only Domino's managed to show some adaptation to the price-sensitive consumer, thanks to the developments I mentioned earlier.

Now, with the strategic shift brought about by 'Better Get You Some', I believe that Papa John's is aware of its mistake in the promotional mix and is ready to make amends. There are some issues that are preventing the company from unleashing the full potential of its plan at this exact moment. That is the subject of our next chapter.

Transitioning to your own platform involves necessary adjustment pains

Comparable sales declined due to fewer transactions (as discussed in relation to value perceptions). However, the continued growth of the aggregator channel (third-party delivery platforms) grew faster than Papa John's organic channels, which resulted in a significant bottleneck in the average check from digital orders. This issue appeared to persist through Q3 2024 and had an approximately 1% negative impact on comparable sales for that quarter.

In this situation, we can say that yes, the aggregator channels are bringing in incremental sales for Papa, but they are not compensating for the reduced average check due to the high delivery and service fees. This is a recurring problem for companies that are leveraging their digital sales. Potbelly (PBPB), for example, has found success by driving digital sales through 'Perks', its loyalty app with instant rewards. Many guests could redeem cookies or toppings for their sandwiches with just a few points. This encouraged not only the migration to the owned channels, but also the repeat guest experience.

When one of the largest FSR chains, Olive Garden, decided to focus on third-party deliveries and partnered with Uber Eats, the company did not give up on using its own platform. This way, it can guarantee an attractive average check on these incremental sales while keeping them at a controlled price and boosting overall sales. Looking at the case of Darden and Olive Garden, UberEats maintains a lower delivery fee than the three third-party delivery giants at $1.87 on average per order. This is about $0.60 lower than GrubHub (OTCPK:TKAYF) and $0.35 lower than DoodDash (DASH). Looking at service fees (which is what concerns Papa John's the most and makes it migrate to its organic platform), UberEats has the highest fee ($4.51), followed by DoorDash ($3.63) and GrubHub ($3.21).

This service fee is the biggest detriment to the average check from digital orders and a big reason why Papa John's is losing the 'Battle of the Pizzas.' That’s not to say Domino’s isn’t leaning on third-party delivery. It is, and it is, heavily. About 3% of total sales came from UberEats in Q3 2024. But the key is that these customers are incremental, not the bulk of orders. I see Domino’s leveraging its rewards program through value promotions and LTOs as a driver of its owned channel, something that should start happening at Papa John’s soon.

In July, the company released an update to its ‘Papa Rewards’ app that improves calls to action and navigation, elevates images and highlights features, and adjusts royalty structures on digital offers to facilitate faster activations. That's likely to be a focus for Papa John's in the second half of the year, but we don't have much of a clue as to how the company will pull it off right now. With these two levers pulled (value and boosting of owned digital channels) at the same time, I can see some improvement while new marketing actions support this. Since everything is so recent, I would not expect changes for Q3 2024, but only for the beginning of FY 2025.

Valuation

There's no denying that Papa John's looks cheap. The stock was trading at similar lows in February 2016 (the biggest gap between eating out and eating at home in recent memory), August 2018, all of 2019, and the start of the pandemic in 2020. But to really assess the upside potential, we need to apply some quantitative methods to calculate whether there really is a discount or not.

Let's start with the 'comps' model. Here I sought to use companies that are or have in their portfolio LSR restaurants at least similar to Papa John's (predominantly franchise model, volume and convenience-based operations and scale). The five companies chosen were: Domino's Pizza, YUM! Brands, Restaurant Brands International (QSR), McDonald's (MCD) and Jack In The Box (JACK). For the metrics I chose the following ratios: P/E, EV/EBITDA, P/CF, EV/EBIT. The results were as follows:

Note that most metrics give us prices that range around $70. The average of these four ratios gives us a PT (Comps) of $73.89. Note that I left out metrics that involve sales such as P/S and EV/S that indicate exorbitant prices given the high valuation of comparable companies in relation to these parameters. This means an upside of approximately 35% of the share value considering the price at which it is traded today on the market.

Looking at the DDM models, we will not need to use the Modigliani-Miller Dividend Irrelevance Hypothesis for firm value since the company has been a dividend payer since 2013. Here the logic is to imagine the value of a stock as the present value of future cash flows to the shareholder. Let's start with the Gordon Model, which assumes that there will be a perpetual dividend stream, ideal for mature companies that are good dividend payers.

Considering the cost of capital to the shareholder as 7.24%, based on the Risk Free Rate + Beta x ERP, and considering the annual dividend flow for FY 2024 ($1.84) growing at a constant rate of 4.75% (the average of the company's last five years) the PT would be $73.90. Now, if we consider a scenario in which the dividend grows at 4% (as in the last three years) the PT would be $56.79. The average of the two target prices would be $65.34, about 20% above the current share price. Currently and with the same cost of capital, the implied dividend growth rate is approximately 3.85%, below historical levels. This may indicate a discount on the stock based on the assumptions that dividends would grow at their historical rate.

Now considering the SPDDM, a strategy in which we hold the stock for a year and sell it the following year, pocketing the dividends paid along the way, Papa John's does not seem so discounted due to the expected P/E scenario for the end of FY 2024. Using the same cost of capital for the shareholder and the dividend for the fiscal year that we already used in the Gordon Model, we are left with calculating the exit price. To do this, let's first consider the base scenario. Here the exit P/E will be 23.78 and the EPS of 2.29, as per the analyst consensus. This will give us an exit price of $54.46. Therefore, there will be two cash flows, the first from D1 ($1.84) and the second the sale price of the stock ($54.46). Discounting this to the present value, the target price for the expected scenario is $52.50. Let's modify the EPS assumptions to model the bull and bear scenarios. For the bear case, let's assume an EPS of $2.09. This would give us an exit price of $49.22 and a target price of $47.62. For the bull case, let's assume the EPS grows to $2.65. This would result in an exit price of $63.02 and a target price of $60.48. An average of these three scenarios would give us a PT (SPDDM) of $53.53.

The average of these three metrics gives us a target price of $64.25. This represents an upside of almost 20% from the current price level.

My Recommendation

Looking at the risk/reward that Papa John's stock seems to pose, I'm on the team that believes the company will be able to make some noise with its value proposition now geared towards today's consumer environment. I don't think the company will emerge victorious from the 'Battle of the Pizzas', but given the potential upside I see a possibility that the stock price will follow the positive developments of new developments.

I say this because the following three fronts are being worked on:

  1. The shift in advertising campaigns to price points rather than focusing on ingredient quality shows that Papa John's will continue to focus on value promotions to attract the average LSR guest, who is highly influenced by this factor, even more so now;
  2. The problem of digital sales growth in aggregator channels seems to be being addressed by Papa Rewards, which is targeting quick rewards to increase purchase occasions. This is the right formula for this, it just needs time and a few incremental dollars to start generating appeal;
  3. Menu innovations like the Cheesy Burger Pizza and New York Style pizza are just the beginning of a pipeline of products that should increase Papa John's appeal to casual guests, especially premium guests with higher ticket prices. Combining innovations with exclusive LTOs in Papa Rewards also seems like a solution that will work for both problems.

That said, I rate Papa John's as a 'Buy' today. I definitely don't see the company taking the lead in the 'Battle of the Pizzas' in the medium term, but it does appear to be a profitable company that was experiencing headwinds that are now being overcome. This should unlock the stock's potential as it moves forward.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Off-Strip Domination: Why Red Rock Is My Favorite Hotel And Casino In Vegas
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Red Rock Resorts (NYSE:RRR) is poised for significant growth, driven by its undervalued real estate portfolio, which includes expansive developed and undeveloped properties in the rapidly expanding Las Vegas local market. The company's gaming-centric business model, considered best-in-class, allows it to dominate the second-largest U.S. gaming market with the largest market share among locals. Led by an experienced management team, Red Rock is well-positioned to capitalize on its operational efficiency and untapped development opportunities. With strong growth potential and underappreciated assets, RRR represents a compelling investment opportunity.

Business Overview

Red Rock Resorts develops and operates casino and entertainment properties in the United States, primarily through two segments, Las Vegas Operations and Native American Management. They own and operate 6 gaming and entertainment facilities and 12 smaller casinos in the Las Vegas regional market. The principal source of revenue is gaming at 67%, where 80-85% of casino revenue is generated from slot play. Their closest competitor, Caesars, is at 27%. Red Rock continues to create value and succeed through its organic growth pipelines. They hold over 522 acres in a land bank valued at over $1B. Red Rock has 100% ownership of all development real estate, and all historic greenfield properties developed by RRR have produced a 20%+ IRR.

Earnings

RRR posted Q2 earnings where net revenues were $486.4mm, a 16.9% increase from Q2 2023. Property EBITDA was up 15% YoY with the main source being the newly developed Durango property, which opened in early December 2023. In Q2 the Durango property increased visitation by 88% while gaining 55,000 new customers to its database. Durango is on track to become one of RRR’s highest EBITDA margin properties. Management noted they experienced positive YoY growth as they targeted “high performance in its VIP section,” which was an intentional move to attract a higher network customer alongside its regional and national targets.

Unit Economics

My investment thesis is predicated on three main points. First, Red Rock has pure-play exposure to the attractive Las Vegas local market, with a pole position to benefit from growth in a more affluent population. More than 70% of the population residing in Clark County (population of 2.3mm) is located within 3 miles of a Red Rock-owned property or greenfield site. This is based on steady, high-quality demand growth, driven by steady population growth skewing to higher-income, higher-net-worth individuals seeking a lower tax rate and a lower cost of living. RRR assets are of higher quality than any of their peers because of their geographic position, they can use this as pricing power and can use it as a lever for revenue growth. The revenue for the entire Las Vegas strip is $7.9B and the local market is $3B. Nevada law (SB 208) significantly limits the development of casinos off the famed Strip and Red Rock occupies most of that real estate. I believe this is why Red Rock has sustained superior growth relative to its peers over the years.

In Las Vegas, there is a low-risk/high-return development pipeline that provides an attractive growth runaway. RRR's undeveloped land bank is underappreciated, as land sale comps don't give credit to the irreplaceable nature of the attached (scarce) gaming licenses coupled with their strategic locations. Its newest property Durango, opened in December 2023. Durango's success will re-cement management credibility with new-build development and offer investors a blueprint to value RRR's strategically positioned land bank. Projections for Durango (Phase 1) adding $145m in EBITDA in 2025, an 18% lift to the existing portfolio, while Phase 2 of Durango could boost the property's returns by 40%.

With Las Vegas being cheaper in terms of cost of living and #1 for new corporate investment in the U.S., Red Rock is in the best position to take advantage of this. Red Rock has a superior capital structure with an interest coverage of 4.48x demonstrating that the company can endure a high-rate environment with current income levels. With a 19% ROIC, they have sustained a consistent 40% EBITDA margin for the last 15 quarters.

Personal income in the Las Vegas Metropolitan area is projected to have reached $145B in 2023; incomes expanded by 6.4% CAGR during the past decade and 5.3% during the past 20 years. This positive tailwind and growth in Las Vegas has allowed Red Rock to invest in its off-strip properties and capitalize on this underserved market.

Growth Strategy

Red Rock’s main growth driver in its pipeline is their North Folk development. RRR has acquired 305 acres adjacent to Highway 99 between Fresno, CA and Madera, CA with the capacity to host 81,000 vehicles per day. 5.8 million people currently live within 2 hours of this site and Red Rock plans to house 2478 slots and 42 table games at this property. It is important to note that the first phase of this development does not involve a hotel. RRR will receive a 30% management fee on net revenue and a 4% development fee for this property. There were negotiations with the Tribe about this development, however, this has been resolved and RRR can move forward with the project. There are favorable tax implications without a negotiated compact (contract between two or more states) and this will directly benefit margins. The 30% management fee Red Rock will collect will roughly contribute to 40% of the property's EBITDA. I believe this is where RRR separates itself. Its business model travels very well and can attack the local markets better than any competitor while sustaining high EBITDA margins.

Risks

The first risk is that a macro (recession) pullback threatens to derail domestic demand. Persistent levels of heightened inflation pose a risk to consumer demand for discretionary spending. Increased costs to the consumer for staples (gas, rent, food) pressure their ability to spend on leisure activities. However, with recent rate cuts and inflation cooling, I don’t see this as a major risk to operations.

The second risk is regarding changes to Las Vegas restrictions on new gaming enterprises could pressure existing operators. Any changes that ease the existing restrictions on new gaming capacity in the local market could garner new supply into the area, and likely negatively impact existing properties for operators such as RRR.

Finally, labor inflation could weigh on margins, while land-based promotions could return to some extent. Elevated levels of wage inflation pose a risk to the heightened margins seen in the gaming industry since operators re-opened their doors from pandemic-related shutdowns. Although, with a 2-3-year period since the pandemic, this hasn’t been a concern for management.

Valuation

I found my cost of equity using the CAPM model accompanied by the 30-year treasury bill as my risk-free rate and added it to the beta of 1.05. I multiplied it by the equity risk premium, which is the TTM S&P 500 return subtracted from my 30-year treasury bill risk-free rate.

The cost of debt of 5% was pulled from Bloomberg.

To calculate my WACC, I multiplied them by the % of equity and % of debt in the capital structure to arrive at 8.2% as my discount rate.

Revenue projections are in line with consensus estimates, which slow, steady growth over the years to come. There is some compression in the later years of the model acting as a conservative approach to how new development properties will perform once complete. RRR has proven to maintain strong EBITDA margins, however, I wanted to illustrate that if margins were to compress about 5% from the 40%+ margins, there is still upside to be had. I forecast current liabilities will see a significant increase with the debt put on the company from the North Folk and the final stages of project Durango. Sticking to this theme, analysts have RRR’s EV/EBITDA at 10.5x however I wanted to stay more in line with peers in the 7-9x ballpark.

Conclusion

In summary, Red Rock Resorts (NASDAQ:RRR) offers strong market positioning and substantial growth potential. Its strategic land bank, well-managed real estate portfolio, and market supremacy among Las Vegas locals offer a strong basis for future growth. RRR's distinct assets, operational prowess, and strong balance sheet position it to outperform its competitors and generate significant value for investors as the Las Vegas market grows.

data.title: DiamondRock Hospitality: Wait For A Dip In The 8.25% Preferred Shares
data.description:
Introduction

DiamondRock Hospitality (NYSE:DRH) is a hotel REIT owning 36 properties with almost 10,000 rooms across the United States. About a third of its EBITDA was generated in Chicago, Boston and New York City, and interestingly, DiamondRock prefers its hotels to be independently managed rather than belonging to one of the larger brands. This reduces the guest flow from guests loyal to certain brands and loyalty programs but it also allows for a different pricing mechanism.

In my previous articles I had a closer look at the REIT’s preferred shares and the preferred equity also is the focus of this article as the share price of the preferred shares is trading at about 4% above the call price, with the initial call date coming pretty close.

The hotel business is still doing fine with an anticipated $200M+ in AFFO this year

As I’m looking at DiamondRock Hotels from the perspective of a preferred shareholder, I will focus on two specific elements: Are the preferred dividends well covered, and is there a major balance sheet risk involved with owning (senior) equity in the REIT?

A good starting point is the FFO and AFFO result generated by the REIT. As you can see below, DiamondRock posted total FFO of $50.1M in the second quarter of this year, while the adjusted FFO was approximately $72.5M as there were quite a few non-recurring items that negatively impacted the reported results.

This means the AFO increased by almost 8% in the second quarter of the year while the increase was just 3% in the first semester as the first quarter is traditionally relatively weak.

The hotel REIT also published its full-year guidance, and as you can see below, it expects to generate a full-year AFFO of $201.5M-$213.5M. Keep in mind the starting point of the FFO and AFFO calculation and guidance is the net income, and as shown below (and above), it already takes the preferred dividend payments into account. DiamondRock expects to pay $9.82M in preferred dividends this year, which means it needs less than 5% of the pre-dividend AFFO to cover the preferred dividend payments.

So from the perspective of the dividend coverage ratio, I don’t see any issues. And looking at the balance sheet, you see there’s $1.64B in equity on the balance sheet, of which $119% is preferred equity.

This means there's in excess of $1.5B in common equity, which ranks junior to the preferred shares. This also provides a decent safety net.

I consider the preferred shares a term investment

DiamondRock only has one series of preferred shares outstanding, a cumulative issue, offering an annual preferred dividend of $2.0625 per preferred share paid in four quarterly installments. The securities, trading with (NYSE:DRH.PR.A) as ticker symbol, can be called from Aug. 31 next year on. And as I'm expecting interest rates in the financial markets to decrease between now and the summer of next year, I think it’s safe to say we should probably consider an investment in DiamondRock as a 10.5-month term investment. That being said, there's of course absolutely no guarantee DiamondRock will call these preferred shares in August next year, but the longer they wait, the higher the annualized return will be as the current premium will be amortized over a longer period.

As you can see in the image above, the current share price of DiamondRock preferred shares is $25.92, which means it's trading at a premium of almost 4% to the call price of $25/share.

The preferred shares just paid a preferred dividend on Sept. 30, which means an investor should be able to expect three more quarterly preferred dividend payments as well as a pro rata dividend payment for two months. This means an investor can expect to receive at least $1.71875 in payments on top of the repayment of the $25 principal resulting in a total payment of $26.7185. That’s a total return of 3.08% on the current investment for a 10.5 month period. Even on an annualized basis, the return on the preferred shares would be lower than what an investor could earn on a one-year US Treasury Bill (currently 4.21% per year).

Of course the scenario above solely focuses on DiamondRock calling the preferred shares at the first opportunity it has. If the preferred shares would only be called in the summer of 2026, the yield to call would increase to approximately 6% assuming a call in June 2026.

Investment thesis

This means that if you believe the preferred shares will be called in August 2025, selling the preferred shares would be the right call (subject of course to transaction fees and the impact of taxes) as the total return until the call date is pretty low. However, if you anticipate the preferred shares will remain outstanding beyond August 2025, it definitely makes sense to hold on to the position as the capital loss upon a call can be amortized over a longer period.

The common shares of DiamondRock are also still relatively interesting, but I have no position in those common shares. I also have no position in the preferred shares anymore.

data.title: Kraft Heinz: Undervalued And Thriving In The Post-3G Era
data.description:
There's wisdom in buying and holding on to companies with household brands that resonate with consumers. Perhaps that's why Warren Buffett's portfolio in Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A)(BRK.B) is chock-full of strong brands that have stood the test of time.

This brings me to Kraft Heinz (NASDAQ:KHC), which remains a top holding in Berkshire Hathaway. As of reporting in August, Buffett still holds 326 million shares in KHC, and unlike his recent culling of shares in Apple (AAPL) and Bank of America (BAC), he did not sell a single share of KHC in the second quarter.

I last covered KHC back in July, highlighting its supply chain efficiencies, price increases to offset margin pressure, and material undervaluation. It appears that the market has agreed to my thesis, with the stock giving investors a 14% total return since my last piece, which is double the 7% rise in the S&P 500 (SPY) over the same timeframe.

The recent 50 basis point reduction in interest rates by the Federal Reserve may have given presumed slower growth stocks like those in consumer staples a bid, as lower rates make their dividends more appealing. Since my last piece, the SPDR Consumer Staples ETF (XLP) has outperformed the S&P 500, and KHC has outperformed even XLP with a 12.5% share price appreciation, as shown below.

In this article, I revisit KHC including recent business performance and discuss why it remains a solid pick for potentially strong long-term returns for income and value investors, so let's get started!

Why KHC?

Kraft Heinz is one of the largest food companies in the world, with a presence in 190 countries and a host of iconic brands in Heinz Ketchup, Kraft Mac & Cheese and condiments, Oscar Mayer, Philadelphia cream cheese, and more.

Those who follow the company know that outsized influence that the Brazilian private equity firm, 3G Capital, had over KHC starting in 2015, when it and Berkshire Hathaway merged the Heinz Company with Kraft. In typical PE fashion, 3G sought to cut costs in order to squeeze higher profits, but that came at the expense of a lack of brand innovation.

3G began pulling back from its ownership stake in 2019 and in 2023 exited its remaining 16% stake in the company. At the same time, KHC has been ramping investments behind its brands to reinvigorate growth. This strategy appears to be working, as KHC's volume declines have slowed down for 4 consecutive quarters. As shown below, KHC's volume decline would be -2.5% had it not been for the impact from temporary plant closure and a rebranding effort currently underway on Lunchables.

While total volume did decline by 3.4% during Q2 2024, as shown above, KHC was able to largely offset that with price increases, resulting in revenue declining by just 0.5% YoY during the quarter. Also encouraging, KHC is seeing growth in gross margins through operational efficiencies. This is reflected by adjusted gross margin rising by 190 basis points on a YoY basis to 35.0% for the first half of this year.

Higher margins contributed to 9% YoY growth in free cash flow to $100 million during the second quarter. This helped to enable $1.5 billion in capital returns to shareholders during the first half of this year, including $969 million worth of dividends paid and $350 million in share repurchases. KHC initiated its share buyback program in Q4 of last year, and over the past 3 reported quarters, has retired a meaningful 1.5% of the outstanding float.

Going forward, management seeks to close the gap with competitors from a pricing perspective and plans to make investments behind 30-40% of its brands. This includes brand innovation, such as the one behind the recent launch of new Mac & Cheese products with different shapes and flavors. Innovations also include partnerships with Taco Bell and Delimex to offer new Mexican-inspired meal solutions to drive sustained long-term growth. Also importantly, KHC is expanding its Away from Home category by improving service levels and expanded distribution.

Looking ahead to Q3 results, I would look for an update on the Away from Home category, as management expects to build momentum during the second half of this year. Moreover, I'd like to see progress toward innovation around the Lunchables and Capri Sun categories, both of which have been in the headlines as of late due to an increasingly health-conscious environment regarding processed foods and packaging. Recent developments include better brand positioning and product evolution, which includes the introduction of a bottle form of Capri Sun.

Meanwhile, KHC continues to deleverage the balance sheet with the aforementioned strong free cash flow generation, reducing the adjusted debt-to-equity ratio by 0.1x on a YoY basis to a 3.0x level, which is on par with a safe level generally considered safe by ratings agencies, supporting its BBB investment grade credit rating.

Importantly for income investors, KHC yields 4.4% and the dividend is well-covered by a 54% payout ratio. While dividend growth has been lacking as of late, share repurchases seem to be a good use of capital returns at the current discounted price of $36.00 with a forward PE of 11.9.

I continue to find KHC to be good value at the current price, given my expectations that the company will return to meaningful growth as it moves past its "3G era" of cost-cutting and low brand investments, with new brand innovation growing by 60 bps as a percentage of sales to 2.4% since the start of the year.

With a well-covered 4.4% yield and sell-side analysts estimates for 5% to 7% annual EPS growth over the next 3 years, KHC could reasonably deliver market-level total returns over the medium term. Over the long-run, I would expect for KHC to be able to trade at a 15-18x PE valuation, which is in-line with that of strong consumer staples stocks as it continues to make progress toward deleveraging the balance sheet.

Risks to the thesis include higher-than-expected cost inflation, which could negatively impact KHC's margins should it not be able to keep up with price increases. Moreover, competition from private label brands remains a potential headwind, as that introduces pricing pressure. Moreover, an increasingly health-conscious consumer base could mean that rebranding efforts on items like Lunchables may fall flat with consumers and not generate the desired growth.

Investor Takeaway

Kraft Heinz remains a solid investment opportunity for income and value investors, supported by a well-covered 4.4% dividend yield and a strong focus on brand innovation and operational efficiency post-3G Capital's cost-cutting era.

Despite modest volume declines, KHC has managed to offset this through strategic price increases and improved margins, with a high single-digit rise in free cash flow and robust capital returns to shareholders. With a forward P/E of 11.9, a commitment to deleveraging, and estimated 5-7% EPS growth by analysts over the next three years, KHC is well-positioned for potentially strong long-term total returns from the current price.

data.title: Avista: Slightly Undervalued, Significant Leverage And Better Protection With This Strategy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In a previous article about Evergy (EVRG) I explained the reasons why six to nine months ago I became increasingly interested in utilities: expectations for lower interest rates (process that has already started) and increased demand of electricity due to AI, electric vehicles and other sources.

And while utilities already had a significant jump in the meantime, we can still find some interesting cases. For regulated utilities, I look for undervalued stocks with a combined dividend yield plus dividend growth of at least 8%.

One of the companies that qualifies is Avista Corporation (NYSE:AVA), a regulated electric and natural gas utility with a dividend yield of about 5% and a dividend growth close to 4%. That is slightly different from Evergy, which offers a dividend yield a bit over 4% and dividend growth close to 5%.

Adding a slight undervaluation to the equation, and the stock might be interesting as a defensive position in a diversified portfolio. For these cases of slightly undervalued low volatility stocks, I prefer a Bull Call (or Put) Spread options strategy because it brings significant leverage and better protection to the downside.

Business Analysis and Economic Moat

Avista is involved in the production, transmission and distribution of energy and some other marginal energy-related businesses. They claim to be one of the cleanest utilities when it comes to greenhouse gases, balancing reliability with renewable energy sources, while managing costs for customers.

The company provides electricity and natural gas to some areas spread across several states:

  • Washington, where they have a pending multi-year electric and natural gas rate case filed in January 2024.
  • Idaho, where they have an approved settlement designed to increase annual base electric revenues by 8%, effective in September 2023, and by 1.7% effective in September 2024; for natural gas they have an agreed increase of 2.7% effective in September 2023.
  • Oregon, where they have an approved settlement designed to increase annual base natural gas revenues by 9.4% effective January 2024.
  • Alaska, where they have an order which results in an approved base electric revenue increase of 6%, effective in October 2023.

The number of electric retail customers increased YoY in the first six months ending June 2024 by about 1.3%, while the number of natural gas retail customers increased in the same period by about 0.8%, based especially on the residential segment. Population growth in Idaho is a contributing factor and is expected to continue the ascending trend through 2029. Oregon also fares well with estimated population growth until 2030.

While the number of commercial and industrial customers is relatively flat YoY, the electric revenues from these segments did increase, helped by the new rates. I also think we could see positive surprises from these segments based on the increased demand of electricity I was talking about in the introduction.

Based on the above, we can conclude that Avista, as most regulated utilities, has an economic moat with the source of the monopoly that they have in certain areas. But again, we can say that this moat is only a weak (or narrow) one, a powerful moat being very difficult due to the limits that the regulators impose on them.

Financial Analysis and Dividend

Avista's financial position is in good shape, with about 5.4x Net Debt / EBITDA. With a BBB credit rating, negative outlook from S&P Global, they score a notch under the average for US regulated utilities, and their interest coverage is a little under the average. However, I think it's possible to see some positive evolutions based on the last rate base increases and lower interest rates.

According to their latest earnings presentation, they expect a rate base growth of 5% though 2026, and I expect that to be continued based on the increased demand of electricity:

In terms of profitability, I think we can expect between 4% and 6% annual EPS growth through 2026, depending on various factors like their pending rate case in Washington, increased residential customers, amplitude of interest rate cuts. Positive surprises might come from the commercial and industrial segments. For example, here is an announcement from the last earnings report:

Effective Aug. 1, we added a large electric customer in our service territory previously served by the wholesale markets. The increase in utility margin due to the addition of this customer is expected to offset substantially all of the forecast impact of higher power supply costs on results in 2024.

The dividend has a current yield of about 5%, but dividend growth is not so strong: ~4.5% in 2023 and ~3.3% in 2024. I expect the growth next years to stay in this range, due to a relatively elevated payout ratio at about 77%, considering their estimated 2024 EPS of 2.46 at the midpoint.

Fair Value

Let's see where Avista is positioned against some comparable regulated US utilities:

Looking at Price / Book, one of my favorite multiples for regulated utilities, with 1.18 Avista has more than 40% discount compared to the average. That's partially explained by their Return on Equity = ~7.84%, about 20% lower than peers, but the difference indicates undervaluation. The EV / EBITDA multiple has again a discount of about 13% versus peers.

But since this comparison cannot say anything if the entire industry is over or under-valued, let's take a look at Avista's five years averages: Price / Book = 1.33, about 12% more than today, and EV / EBITDA = 11.95, about the same 12% more than today.

For an absolute valuation, I use a DDM (Discounted Dividend Model) in two stages: about five years with higher dividend growth, then a lower dividend growth in perpetuity. Here are my assumptions for Avista:

Div growth

Compared with the current stock price (37.98), the DDM shows an undervaluation of about 13%, which is in line with the relative valuation.

In conclusion, this is what Avista could bring us from a stock position:

  • 12-13% return from the discount to fair value (this has a degree of uncertainty, of course, even if it's low for a regulated utility)
  • ~5% dividend yield
  • 3 to 4% dividend growth

While this is not shy for a defensive position, I look at options for both better returns and better protection. If some investors, for any reason, don't want to get involved with options, they can skip the next section.

Significant Leverage and Better Protection Through Options

Like for Evergy, I chose a Bull Call (or Put) Spread strategy. There are several reasons why this strategy: the stock is slightly undervalued, but without significant potential, while this strategy brings significant leverage; low volatility makes selling options less appealing; limited loss profile compared to stocks or other strategies. For beginners completely unfamiliar with options, I do encourage them to read, but I recommend testing this strategy first on a demo account for about one year.

For a Bull Call Spread, if the short Call is In-The-Money with very low extrinsic value, we could be assigned just before the ex-dividend date, which translates to short 100 shares instead of a short Call, which will cost us about $49 dividend. While this is a manageable situation (like I described in my article about Evergy), since Call Spreads and Put Spreads have a similar profile, let's see a Bull Put Spread in this case.

For a Bull Put spread, we could still be assigned exactly after the ex-dividend date if the short Put is In-The-Money with low extrinsic value, which translates to long 100 shares instead of short Put. But that's not very problematic because we could immediately close the position, with minor changes regarding the profit/loss.

For example, we can sell an AVA 40 Put Mar '25 for about $325, and we can buy an AVA 35 Put for about $105. Total cost is 500 (the spread between the Puts, which is the maximum cash that I need to pay at expiration if both Puts finish In-The-Money) - 220 (difference between premiums that I collected) = about $280.

The maximum profit at expiration would be about $220 for stock price above $40, meaning 78.5%, or about 188% annualized. The break-even is at about $37.8, and maximum loss is the total cost for stock price under $35.

Let's see how we can handle this position in different scenarios to profit even at lower prices:

Scenario 1: Stock price above $40 (this is my base case considering expectations for lower interest rates in about six months, that's why I chose March 2025 as expiration date):

Let the position collect the maximum profit, that could be ~188% annualized at expiration or even higher if we close it before. For example, if we could close the position in February 2025 for $15, it's not worth it to keep it more (we avoid a possible price reversal), and we can gather a profit of (220 - 15) / 280 = about 73%, or 219% annualized.

Scenario 2: Stock price retreats to low thirties in 2-3 months, then it recovers only about half the loss next year, settling at about $35 (possible if interest rate cuts are delayed for a few months):

It's better to close the position while our long 35 Put is at around $35 and still has extrinsic value, 2-3 months before expiration. Otherwise, with stock price under $35 at expiration, we'll get the maximum loss as in the chart above.

In a second iteration, with the stock price under $35, we could open another Put Spread at lower strikes, short 35 Put, long 30 Put, expiration June '25, which is very similar to our initial position, maybe at a slightly higher cost.

Then, with stock price at around $35, in a third iteration we can just roll over the same position (short 35 Put, long 30 Put):
