In this scenario, the total cost (maximum funds needed to be set aside) is a bit higher since we need extra funds after the loss in the first iteration in order to open the second iteration, but in about one year we will still have a significant return of about 195/460 = 42.3%, and that even if the stock price is lower, at $35!

Scenario 3: The stock price retreats to the low thirties in 2–3 months, then it keeps heading down to under $30 (not very likely, but still possible scenario in case of higher interest rates for much longer):

The second iteration described above will finish with another loss, we'll have a total loss of about $300.

Now, with the stock in the high twenties, Avista would be significantly undervalued, and we could switch to a more aggressive strategy, to counterbalance the effect of this combined loss and, in most cases, still bring us a consistent profit. Buying a Call option is such a strategy, but contrary to some popular opinions that a Call (or Put) Spread is more difficult (because it has two legs compared to one for a single Call), in fact, buying a Call is a more difficult strategy to implement because there are several intricacies, so that might be a subject for another article. Or even owning the stock is almost always a good choice when it's significantly undervalued.

Finally, even in the very unlikely scenario of Avista heading to bankruptcy in 2–3 months, we still want the Put (or Call) spread due to its limited loss profile, while owning the stock would lead to much higher losses.

Risks

The obvious interest rate risk is mitigated, or sometimes nullified, by the active management of the Put Spread before expiration and the position rollover, like I described above in Scenario 2 and Scenario 3.

Extreme weather can provoke some damage to utilities. While the US West Coast is not very exposed to hurricanes, there is an increasing risk of wildfires due to higher temperatures. Idaho is one of the states with a high risk of wildfires, especially from mid-summer to autumn. But Avista is taking active measures, one of their top 3 capital programs 2024-2026 is "Wildfire resiliency", as presented above. Their subsidiary, Alaska Electric & Power, faces the risk of avalanches, and they are implementing an avalanche diversion project to mitigate that risk.

Related to the Put Spread strategy, Avista Put options are not very liquid. If someone wants to open a large number of Put spreads, there can be problems regarding the spread, so higher costs. In this case, switching to the similar Call spread strategy might help, I see that Open Interest is higher for Calls than for Puts. If there are still problems with getting decent prices, opening several smaller positions instead of a big one not only that alleviates this, but also can offer even better protection because we could add new Call (or Put) spread positions at better prices if the stock price moves down.

Takeaway

Avista, like most utilities, will benefit from lower interest rates and from an increased demand of electricity. With regulated electricity and natural gas operations in Washington, Idaho, Oregon, Alaska, the company has a weak (or narrow) economic moat.

They are targeting 5% rate base growth through 2026, and I expect a 4 to 6% EPS growth. The dividend yield is 5%, but with a relatively high payout ratio of about 77%, I expect lower annual dividend growth, likely between 3 and 4%.

Avista seems 12-13% undervalued, based on both a relative valuation and an absolute one. A two-stages Discounted Dividend Model (with 3.75% dividend growth next 5 years, 3% growth in perpetuity and 7.7% Discount rate) gives a Fair Value = 43.05 vs. 37.98 stock price today.

For significant leverage and better protection, I chose a bullish 40/35 Put spread strategy. The strategy could bring close to 200% annualized return with stock price over $40, or about 42% return even with the price retreating towards low thirties in 2-3 months, and then settling at ~$35. If the price continued to head down, I would recommend switching to a more aggressive strategy at that moment (because the stock would be significantly undervalued at those levels) to counter-balance the initial losses. Even in an unlikely scenario of Avista heading to bankruptcy, this strategy is much better than simply owning the stock, due to its limited loss profile.

data.title: Celestica: Riding The Wave Of Unstoppable AI Momentum
data.description:
Introduction

I am pleased to report that Celestica's (NYSE:CLS) (TSX:CLS:CA) share price grew by 40% since I covered it in April for the first time. I hope this was beneficial for readers who decided to opt in based on my call.

Celestica is an interesting stock and definitely deserves a thesis update, especially considering the upcoming earnings release which is planned for October 23. Tailwinds remain very strong for Celestica, as several recent developments suggest that spending for data centers is still very strong, and this momentum is not getting weaker. If recent months showed us that only tech giants were investing vast amounts in building new data centers, now we see some prominent asset managers also expanding into this field. Celestica has been strong so far in absorbing AI tailwinds, and I see no reasons for this trend to terminate. My DCF model suggests that the stock is almost 40% undervalued, which makes it a 'Strong Buy' again.

Fundamental analysis

Before I delve into my expectations around the upcoming earnings release, let me remind readers that CLS is enjoying the AI race among cloud companies and has demonstrated a strong revenue and EBITDA growth so far this year.

According to SA, Celestica will release its Q3 earnings on October 23 after the market close. Since Celestica was one of the stocks that rallied significantly together with other companies with AI exposure, we need to look at developments around the data center industry. According to delloro.com, 1H2024 worldwide data center capital expenditure grew by 38% on a YoY basis.

There are a few more solid indications that the AI momentum for data centers is not cooling down. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM), has recently released its Q3 2024 earnings. TSM's revenue grew by an impressive 36% YoY, and management's Q4 guidance was notably higher than what consensus expected. If TSM achieves its Q4 revenue guidance of $26.5 billion (midpoint of the range), it will mean a 30% YoY growth.

Nvidia's CEO, Jensen Huang, recently said that the demand for the company's new Blackwell GPU is "insane". Based on the soaring revenue of TSM, the company that produces Nvidia's chips, the demand for Blackwell is indeed quite strong. According to the source, Blackwell is expected to be notably more expensive than the company's previous generation Hopper GPU. If the demand is indeed "insane", this means that data center owners are ready to pay a premium price to ensure technological edge.

With that being said, the momentum for semiconductors is expected to remain very strong at least through the end of 2024. It is also highly likely that the industry will remain hot at least through 2025 as well. I think that we are already used to headlines that Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), and Google (GOOGL) frequently announce their new investments in data centers across the world. The new trend is that asset managers recently also started investing billions in data centers. For example, Blackstone (BX) recently announced that it plans to invest more than $8 billion in data centers in Spain. Blue Owl Capital (OBDC) has recently established a JV that will also invest billions in data centers.

Therefore, AI tailwinds are still very strong, which is beneficial for Celestica. Celestica's accelerated revenue growth starting from FY 2023 suggests a strong ability to capitalize on AI tailwinds. Therefore, it is fair that the trend of consensus EPS revision looks very bullish. Celestica consistently deliver positive earnings surprises, which is another positive factor before the upcoming earnings release.

Valuation analysis

Celestica's forward P/E ratios look quite low because Wall Street analysts expect solid EPS expansion over the next few years. As a result, the FY2026 forward P/E ratio is currently estimated at just 11. In my fundamental analysis, I have explained why CLS is well-positioned to deliver strong EPS expansion in reality. Therefore, CLS looks very attractively valued based on forward P/E ratios.

The discounted cash flow ('DCF') model will help in figuring out the stock's upside potential. Celestica's WACC is around 8%. Revenue assumptions for FYs 2024-2025 are from consensus. For FYs 2026-2028, I project a 50 basis point growth deceleration per year. The TTM FCF margin is 3.2%, which is expected to expand by 25 bps every year. The constant growth rate for terminal value ('TV') calculation is 3%, which I consider fair given Celestica's solid exposure to secular data center tailwinds. According to Seeking Alpha, there are 117.5 million shares outstanding.

According to the DCF, with all aforementioned assumptions incorporated, the stock's fair price is around $81. This represents a 39% upside potential, which I consider compelling, and my assumptions are quite conservative.

Mitigating factors

Buying the stock before the earnings release is risky. The market's reaction can be quite unpredictable. It is common when investors start selling-off the stock after earnings, even if a company delivered a firm positive EPS surprise. For example, CLS reported its Q2 earnings on July 23 with a positive earnings surprise and robust Q3 guidance. Nevertheless, historical price quotes indicate that the stock was -7.7% on July 24 and -4.35% on July 25. Therefore, even if CLS delivers strong Q3 earnings, there is a risk of a temporary pullback.

There is a significant customer concentration risk, according to Celestica's latest annual report. FY 2023 sales from the largest customer represented around 25% of the total. As a result, Celestica's ten largest customers contributed 64% to consolidated sales. High customer concentration is inherently risky, as it makes any company heavily dependent on its partners' decisions and financial strength. Thus, a potential decrease in revenue from one of its largest customers might have a significant adverse effect on the company's P&L.

Conclusion

My previous bullish call aged well, and I expect the updated bullish thesis to be successful as well. Tailwinds are still strong and there are no signs that the AI momentum is cooling down. Celestica has been quite strong in capitalizing on tailwinds, and there is still solid upside left.
